items.question: What is the linear regression model equation?
items.answer: ŷ = wᵀx + b for one example, or ŷ = Xw + b in batch matrix form, where w are learned weights, b is bias, and ŷ predicts continuous y.

items.question: What loss does ordinary least squares minimize?
items.answer: Mean squared error: J(w) = (1/n) Σ(yᵢ − ŷᵢ)². It penalizes squared residuals and is the default training objective for standard linear regression.

items.question: State the normal equation for OLS.
items.answer: XᵀXw = Xᵀy, with solution w* = (XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy when X has full column rank. It comes from setting the gradient of MSE to zero.

items.question: Why can the normal equation fail in practice?
items.answer: Collinear or redundant features make XᵀX singular (non-invertible), so w is not unique. Large or ill-conditioned X also makes direct inversion numerically unstable — use lstsq, QR, or gradient descent.

items.question: How does linear regression relate to maximum likelihood?
items.answer: If y = Xw + ε with Gaussian ε ~ N(0, σ²I), minimizing MSE on w is equivalent to MLE of w. See Maximum Likelihood Estimation for the general derivation.

items.question: When would you use gradient descent instead of the normal equation?
items.answer: When d or n is large (inversion is O(d³)), data arrive streaming, or XᵀX is ill-conditioned. Both methods target the same MSE minimum for full-rank convex problems.
